EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major storm across the Great Lakes remained nearly stationary during the afternoon of April 3rd as a coastal low developed off the NJ-DE that eventually moved to near Cape Cod, MA by midday April 4th. In addition, a strong area of high pressure was located in Hudson Bay but was ridging/cresting toward northern New England and NY.||Windy conditions developed across VT during the afternoon hours of April 3rd and continued into the early morning hours of April 4th. The stronger winds were located across southern VT where some wind gusts exceeded 50-60 mph with approximately 13,000+ outages due to wind.||Mixed rain, sleet and snow developed in the afternoon and evening hours of April 3rd and then transitioned to a heavy wet snow overnight through midday on April 4th. Snowfall totals ranged from 2 to 6 inches along the immediate lakeshore with 6 to 10 inches within 3-5 miles in the Champlain valley. Other snowfall totals were 8 to 12 inches within the immediate Connecticut River Valley with 12 to 20+ inches elsewhere across the state.||Impacts were significant across the region with hundreds of motor vehicle accidents, numerous school closing and more than 35.000 power outages at it's peak due to snow weighted trees on powerlines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy, wet snowfall totals of 10 to 15 inches were common with slightly lower amounts in the deepest valley with higher totals in the higher terrain. Some specific totals include; 20 inches in Waldon, 17 inches in Sutton, 16 inches in Stannard and Wheelock, 14 inches in West Burke, 12 inches in Groton, 10 inches in Danville with 9 inches in St. Johnsbury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak boundary draped across northern NY and northern VT in a marginally unstable airmass led to the development of scattered thunderstorms during the afternoon of May 21st. A few of these thunderstorms intensified to deliver isolated damaging winds and hail up to one inch in diameter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree and wires down along Route 207 between Cook road and Woods Hill road  intersection.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved from the southeast United States on March 22nd up the eastern seaboard to the NJ/DE coast by midday on March 24th and offshore by Cape Cod late at night on March 24th. ||Snow overspread Vermont, south to north, during the early morning hours of March 23rd and then exiting from west to east during the evening hours of March 23rd. The higher snowfall accumulations were across south central and southern VT, except along the MA/VT border where some mixed precipitation types occurred.||Snowfall totals ranged from 3 to 6 inches in northwest VT, 6 to 12 inches across the rest of the Champlain Valley and northern VT, 12 to 18 inches in central VT with 18 to 24+ inches across south central VT with these higher totals mainly across Windsor and eastern Rutland counties.||Impacts were largely travel related.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals were generally 4 to 6 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail reported by spotter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak boundary draped across northern NY and northern VT in a marginally unstable airmass led to the development of scattered thunderstorms during the afternoon of May 21st. A few of these thunderstorms intensified to deliver isolated damaging winds and hail up to one inch in diameter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: West Enosburg road closed due to multiple trees down with poles and power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary surface boundary draped across northern NY/VT, during the evening and overnight hours of January 24th, was the focusing mechanism for moisture that entrained along the boundary and across the area. Temperatures were below freezing across much of central and eastern VT that allowed for several hours of light freezing rain during the evening and overnight hours. Flat ice accumulations were generally 0.1 to 0.2 inches with isolated amounts around 0.25 inches. The main impacts were icy, hazardous travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flat ice accumulations of 0.1 to 0.2 inches were observed in sections of the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak coastal storm moved passed southern New England on January 16th. Light snow overspread Vermont during the morning and ended during the evening. Snowfall totals ranged from 3 to 7 inches across the region with the higher totals in the higher terrain of central VT. The main impacts were numerous traffic accidents, despite the dryness of the snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 4 to 7 inches were observed.

The main impacts from this storm were hazardous road conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 6 to 9 inches were observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary surface boundary draped across northern NY/VT, during the evening and overnight hours of January 24th, was the focusing mechanism for moisture that entrained along the boundary and across the area. Temperatures were below freezing across much of central and eastern VT that allowed for several hours of light freezing rain during the evening and overnight hours. Flat ice accumulations were generally 0.1 to 0.2 inches with isolated amounts around 0.25 inches. The main impacts were icy, hazardous travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flat ice accumulations of 0.1 to 0.2 inches were observed in sections of the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked off the East Coast and brought a period of snow to much of southern Vermont during the daytime hours on January 28, 2024. By the overnight period and into the morning hours on January 29, 2024, precipitation transitioned to a wintry mix and/or eventually plain rainfall.||Snowfall amounts ranged from 2 to 7 inches across much of the area, resulting in locally slippery travel. The Vermont Route 9 Chain-Up Law was also activated between Bennington and Wilmington.||Key impacts: travel delays.

The Vermont Route 9 Chain-Up Law was also activated between Bennington and Wilmington.||Key impacts: travel delays.

Low pressure developed across the northern Gulf of Mexico coast during the evening hours of January 5th and traveled along the east coast moving off the southeast coast of New England by the afternoon of January 7th.||Snow moved into Vermont during the evening hours of January 6th and exited by mid afternoon of January 7th. Snowfall amounts across VT were generally 4 to 12+ inches with the lighter amounts in the northern Champlain Valley and Essex county with the higher snowfall totals *8-12+ inches) across the eastern slope communities of the southern and central Green Mountains. The main impacts from this storm were hazardous road conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 6 to 9 inches were observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracking off the East Coast brought widespread accumulating snow to southern Vermont Monday night, January 15, 2024 through Tuesday evening, January 16, 2024. The event resulted in snowfall accumulations ranging from 2 to 6 inches with isolated, larger accumulations of 7 to 8 inches. The Vermont Route 9 Chain-Up Law was activated between Bennington and Wilmington.||Key impacts: travel delays.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon-evening of June 7th, a weak trough moved into a marginally unstable, cold pool aloft atmosphere in VT that created scattered showers and a few isolated thunderstorms. One thunderstorm produced slightly larger than one inch diameter hail and some gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail reported with some just slightly larger than a quarter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across a moderately unstable airmass across Vermont during the afternoon. Scattered thunderstorms developed in central VT and intensified as they moved east into NH. One thunderstorm caused minor wind damage in two communities in Orange county.

Low pressure across the Carolinas during the afternoon of March 9th moved slowly northeast along the coast across southeast Massachusetts during the morning of March 10th and lingered along the Maine/Nova Scotia border through the early morning hours of March 11th.||The impactful weather occurred in two phases. The first phase occurred during the evening and overnight hours of March 9th with valley rain and elevated mountain wet and heavy snowfall, especially in the central and southern Green Mountain communities. The heavy wet snow greatly impacted the higher terrain and resulted in numerous tree damage, power outages (>30,000 outages) and travel disruptions. Snowfall was less prevalent in the valleys. The second phase occurred during the evening on March 10th through early afternoon on March 11th with northwest upslope enhanced snowfall across the northwest slopes of the central and northern Green Mountains as well as some interior valleys. This, combined with wind gusts in excess of 30 mph led to numerous travel disruptions. During the afternoon hours as the snow waned, northwest winds increased with frequent gusts in excess of 40 mph which led to delays in power restoration.||Storm total snowfall from the evening of March 9th through the afternoon hours of March 11th ranged from 2 to 5 inches across the valley floors of the Champlain and the mid to lower Connecticut River valleys, 4 to 8 inches in elevations above 1000 feet  and 10 to 18+ inches in the higher elevations (1500-2000+ feet), especially the northwest slopes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mixed rain changing to snow occurred with snowfall totals of 3 to 8 inches across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure across the Carolinas during the afternoon of March 9th moved slowly northeast along the coast across southeast Massachusetts during the morning of March 10th and lingered along the Maine/Nova Scotia border through the early morning hours of March 11th.||The impactful weather occurred in two phases. The first phase occurred during the evening and overnight hours of March 9th with valley rain and elevated mountain wet and heavy snowfall, especially in the central and southern Green Mountain communities. The heavy wet snow greatly impacted the higher terrain and resulted in numerous tree damage, power outages (>30,000 outages) and travel disruptions. Snowfall was less prevalent in the valleys. The second phase occurred during the evening on March 10th through early afternoon on March 11th with northwest upslope enhanced snowfall across the northwest slopes of the central and northern Green Mountains as well as some interior valleys. This, combined with wind gusts in excess of 30 mph led to numerous travel disruptions. During the afternoon hours as the snow waned, northwest winds increased with frequent gusts in excess of 40 mph which led to delays in power restoration.||Storm total snowfall from the evening of March 9th through the afternoon hours of March 11th ranged from 2 to 5 inches across the valley floors of the Champlain and the mid to lower Connecticut River valleys, 4 to 8 inches in elevations above 1000 feet  and 10 to 18+ inches in the higher elevations (1500-2000+ feet), especially the northwest slopes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy wet snow accumulated on the night of March 9th that led to power outages across the region. Additional snowfall, more powdery, fell during the night of March 10th through midday March 11th. Storm total snowfall was 8-12+ inches. Some specific snowfall totals include the higher terrain: 13 inches in Shrewsbury and 8 inches in Pittsford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure traveled along the Canadian border while it's associated cold front moved across a moderately unstable airmass across NY/VT. This resulted in scattered thunderstorms developing in the afternoon hours with a few producing damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A clipper system passing to the north of the region on March 20, 2024 lead to some upslope snow showers in southern Vermont. Stronger forcing developed along the associated cold front as the system moved overhead, resulting in a localized occurrence of a snow squall. During the squall, the Bennington ASOS measured visibility of one quarter of a mile and a peak wind gust of 39 mph with thunder in the distance. ||Key Impacts: Travel delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow Squall. Bennington ASOS reported visibilities down to one quarter of a mile with a peak wind gust of 39 mph and thunder in the vicinity. The squall began around 536 pm EDT and ended around 553 pm EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread heavy snow showers and snow squalls tracked across southern Vermont on January 14, 2024 ahead of an approaching arctic cold front. Visibilities reduced to near zero in many of these squalls and winds occasionally gusted between 30 and 45 mph.||The Vermont Route 9 Chain-Up Law was activated between Bennington and Wilmington.||Key impacts: travel delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow squall. Visibilities were reduced to one quarter of a mile with a peak wind gust of 40 mph at the Bennington State Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak trough moved across the international border with Canada during the afternoon of August 14th and triggered a few isolated thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms produced locally heavy rainfall of 1 to 3 inches, which led to a few reports of flash flooding, most notably in the East Burke area where roads damaged in the prior July 2024 flash floods were once again compromised.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Town of St. Johnsbury reported several roads that were damaged during the July 2024 floods were damaged/compromised again. These included Mt. Pisgah Rd., Bible Hill Rd. and New Boston Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms produced locally heavy rainfall of 1 to 3 inches, which led to a few reports of flash flooding, most notably in the East Burke area where roads damaged in the prior July 2024 flash floods were once again compromised.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fire Dept. reported a few roads damaged during the July 2024 floods were damaged/compromised by new flash flooding in the E. Burke area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracking across the Great Lakes region brought a combination of snow, rain, a brief wintry mix and windy conditions to southern Vermont during the overnight hours on January 12 through the daytime hours on January 13, 2024. Wind gusts over 50 mph were the main impact with this system resulting in some downed utility lines across Bennington County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: U.S. Route 7 was closed between exits 3 and 4 due to downed utility lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread heavy snow showers and snow squalls tracked across southern Vermont on January 14, 2024 ahead of an approaching arctic cold front. Visibilities reduced to near zero in many of these squalls and winds occasionally gusted between 30 and 45 mph.||The Vermont Route 9 Chain-Up Law was activated between Bennington and Wilmington.||Key impacts: travel delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow squall. Visibilities reduced to less than one quarter of a mile with a peak wind gust of 44 mph at the Bennington State Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The complex interaction of a northern stream shortwave and a southern stream coastal low pressure system brought widespread snow (moderate to heavy in places), freezing rain, and plain rain to southern Vermont from the evening of Friday, March 22 through Saturday evening, March 23, 2024. The heaviest snow fell across the northern halves of both Bennington and Windham counties. Snowfall totals generally ranged from 5 to 10 inches across southern portions of the counties to 10 to 20 inches across the north with localized amounts in excess of 30 inches along the spine of the Southern Green Mountains. Additionally, some reports of about one quarter of an inch of freezing rain accumulation was reported. ||Nearly 10,000 people lost power during this storm. ||Key Impacts: Power outages, school closures, traffic accidents, travel delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Zone average snowfall of 12.3 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm entered the Pacific NW on January 6th and then traversed the southern Plains on January 8th before moving into the Great Lakes on January 9th-10th. Central pressure fell to 972mb across the eastern Great Lakes during the early morning hours of January 10th, meanwhile a 1026mb area of high pressure was across eastern Quebec/Nova Scotia.||This creating a > 50mb pressure gradient between both systems which created a strong southeast low-level jet of 85-100 mph across much of the northeast including NY and VT. ||Downsloping southeast winds gusted to over 60 mph in some locations across VT that resulted in downed tree limbs, powerlines and approximately 80,000 power outages that resulted in more than $1. million in damages. Several inches of wet snow started the event with localized more than 6 inches across the higher terrain of the Green Mountains and neighboring communities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of 50-55 mph but greater tree damage outside of that area for estimated wind gusts in excess of 60 mph. Numerous power outages as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moisture rich, strong low pressure system brought rain; heavy, wet, high elevation snow; and gusty winds to southern Vermont from Saturday, March 9 through Monday, March 11, 2024.  The heaviest snow fell over the higher elevations of the Southern Green Mountains with snowfall totals between 7 and 15 inches. Snowfall totals of 1 to 5 inches occurred across the lower elevations. Additionally, strong winds  gusting up to 45 mph were reported across southern Vermont. As a result of the heavy snow, the state of Vermont enforced their Chain Up Law along Vermont Route 9. Several crashes due to snow covered roads were also reported. ||Key Impacts: travel delays and traffic accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure slowly tracking up and just off the East Coast brought widespread moderate to heavy snow across southern Vermont on January 6-7, 2024. Snowfall totals were generally between 5 and 10 inches with localized areas around a foot. The Route 9 Truck Chain-up Law was declared for this event. Snow Emergencies were also declared for some municipalities.||Key impacts: travel delays.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system brought gusty winds, locally heavy rainfall, and high elevation wet snow to southern Vermont from the afternoon hours on January 9 into the early morning hours on January 10, 2024. Between 5 and locally 10 inches of snow fell across portions of the southern Greens along with a light accumulation of sleet and freezing rain before changing over to plain rain. The snow resulted in the activation of the Chain-Up Law along Vermont Route 9 between Bennington and Wilmington. On the warmer side of this system, between 2 and 3 inches of rain fall across the Connecticut River Valley but did not result in any widespread flooding. In addition, localized wind gusts between 45 and 55 mph occurred across Bennington County.||Key impacts: travel delays.

Central pressure fell to 972mb across the eastern Great Lakes during the early morning hours of January 10th, meanwhile a 1026mb area of high pressure was across eastern Quebec/Nova Scotia.||This creating a > 50mb pressure gradient between both systems which created a strong southeast low-level jet of 85-100 mph across much of the northeast including NY and VT. ||Downsloping southeast winds gusted to over 60 mph in some locations across VT that resulted in downed tree limbs, powerlines and approximately 80,000 power outages that resulted in more than $1. million in damages. Several inches of wet snow started the event with localized more than 6 inches across the higher terrain of the Green Mountains and neighboring communities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wet snow accumulated 4 to 8 inches across the region with the higher totals mainly at elevations above 1500 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure moved northeast across the Great Lakes on February 28th, but created a strong pressure gradient between this storm and high pressure across the Canadian Maritimes. This allowed strong south-southeast winds ahead of a cold front associated with the strong area of low pressure and southwest winds behind the frontal boundary. The strongest winds occurred along and behind the cold front during the evening and early night hours of February 28th.||Numerous reports of wind gusts in excess of 45 mph with several gusts in excess of 55 mph occurred along with downed tree limbs. branches and subsequent power outages.|Power outages across VT were between 15,000-20,000.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of wind gusts in the 40-50 mph range along with varying types of tree damage that impacted power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of TS Beryl combined with deep tropical moisture and a stationary front draped across the region produced multiple rounds of heavy showers and storms. This led to numerous reports of flash flooding, some locally severe across a roughly 50 mile swath through Chittenden, Washington, Lamoille, Caledonia, Orleans and Essex Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dept. of Highways and the general public reported several roads damaged or washed out in the Richmond and Bolton areas. This was due to flash flooding after 3 to 5 inches of excessive rainfall fell in the affected area. Roads affected included, but were not limited to Route 2 by the Bolton Valley Access Road, and Route 2 in Jonesville at Jones Mill Road. Subsequent storm surveys indicated damage on Besaw Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of TS Beryl combined with deep tropical moisture and a stationary front draped across the region produced multiple rounds of heavy showers and storms. This led to numerous reports of flash flooding, some locally severe across a roughly 50 mile swath through Chittenden, Washington, Lamoille, Caledonia, Orleans and Essex Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public video/picture of severe flash flooding along the Stevens River in Barnet Village. Subsequent reports from Emergency Mgmt. and NWS Storm Surveys the following morning confirmed severe to devastating flash flooding throughout the Barnet and Peacham area with numerous roads and structures heavily damaged or destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of TS Beryl combined with deep tropical moisture and a stationary front draped across the region produced multiple rounds of heavy showers and storms. EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer reported flash flooding along Depot Brook in Groton. NWS Storm surveys the following morning confirmed additional, and in some cases severe flash flooding in Groton, particularly along stretches of US Route 302. Here, several tributaries to the adjacent Wells River damaged the road, driveways and some structures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dangerous heat and humidity occurred on July 10, 2024 across southern Vermont excluding the southern Green Mountains. Heat index values (or feel-like temperatures) reached between 95 and 104 degrees.
Dangerous heat and humidity occurred on July 10, 2024 across southern Vermont excluding the southern Green Mountains. Heat index values (or feel-like temperatures) reached between 95 and 104 degrees.

During the afternoon and early evening hours of July 16, 2024, a strong upper level disturbance developed an organized line of thunderstorms which tracked east through southern Vermont resulting in multiple downed trees and power lines.||A storm survey conducted by the National Weather Service in Bennington County lead to the determination of a microburst in the town of North Rupert. The microburst, reaching an estimated peak wind of 95 mph, attributed to over 2 dozen trees becoming snapped and uprooted over a 0.24 mile long and 50 yard wide damage path at the Old Gray Barn.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local emergency manager reported a downed tree on Vermont Route 30 in the town of Dorset.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and early evening hours of July 16, 2024, a strong upper level disturbance developed an organized line of thunderstorms which tracked east through southern Vermont resulting in multiple downed trees and power lines.||A storm survey conducted by the National Weather Service in Bennington County lead to the determination of a microburst in the town of North Rupert. The microburst, reaching an estimated peak wind of 95 mph, attributed to over 2 dozen trees becoming snapped and uprooted over a 0.24 mile long and 50 yard wide damage path at the Old Gray Barn.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst occurred east of Route 30 just south of North Rupert, VT at the Old Gray Barn. An estimated two dozen trees were downed along the 0.24 mile long and 50 yard wide damage path at the Old Gray Barn location. Trees were noted to be both snapped and uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of nearly stationary thunderstorms affected portions of Caledonia, Essex and Orleans Counties in northeastern Vermont during the early to mid morning hours of July 30, 2024. This produced rainfall amounts from 4 to as much as 8 inches in these areas which led to severe, and in some cases catastrophic flash flooding. The department of highways, state/local emergency management, and NWS Storm Surveys confirmed the hardest hit areas were in the towns of Morgan, Lyndon and St. Johnsbury, VT. Preliminary assessments show five bridges destroyed, five homes destroyed, damage to 20 to 30 additional homes, and numerous roads washed out. Total damage costs will likely near or exceed $10 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dept. of Highways reported US 5 washed out just south of the State Police Barracks. NWS Storm surveys confirmed a significant washout at this location, and along several points on Concord Ave. in St. Johnsbury where locally severe flash flooding occurred at the intersection with Gilman Avenue. Here a bridge was completely destroyed and several homes significantly damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Modest instability under an upper level area of low pressure in the afternoon of July 29th developed a few isolated thunderstorms, including one in Rutland county that produced slightly larger than quarter size hail in Clarendon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail size of slightly larger than a quarter fell across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate instability under an upper level area of low pressure across northern VT during the afternoon of July 30th led to scattered thunderstorms with isolated tree damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of TS Beryl combined with deep tropical moisture and a stationary front draped across the region produced multiple rounds of heavy showers and storms. This led to numerous reports of flash flooding, some locally severe across a roughly 50 mile swath through Chittenden, Washington, Lamoille, Caledonia, Orleans and Essex Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dept. of Highways reported several roads washed out in the Lunenburg area. This included, but was not limited to River Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of nearly stationary thunderstorms affected portions of Caledonia, Essex and Orleans Counties in northeastern Vermont during the early to mid morning hours of July 30, 2024. This produced rainfall amounts from 4 to as much as 8 inches in these areas which led to severe, and in some cases catastrophic flash flooding. The department of highways, state/local emergency management, and NWS Storm Surveys confirmed the hardest hit areas were in the towns of Morgan, Lyndon and St. Johnsbury, VT. Preliminary assessments show five bridges destroyed, five homes destroyed, damage to 20 to 30 additional homes, and numerous roads washed out. Total damage costs will likely near or exceed $10 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Media reported flash flood damage in the towns of Morgan and Charleston. Location of events and times approximate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of nearly stationary thunderstorms affected portions of Caledonia, Essex and Orleans Counties in northeastern Vermont during the early to mid morning hours of July 30, 2024. The department of highways, state/local emergency management, and NWS Storm Surveys confirmed the hardest hit areas were in the towns of Morgan, Lyndon and St. Johnsbury, VT. Preliminary assessments show five bridges destroyed, five homes destroyed, damage to 20 to 30 additional homes, and numerous roads washed out. Total damage costs will likely near or exceed $10 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dept. of Highways reported flash flooding and washouts along Route 2 in St. Johnsbury from Snell Rd. east through Spaulding Rd. and to Pettyco Junction. Other significant damage was reported in the immediate St. Johnsbury area. NWS Storm Surveys also confirmed considerable flash flood damage to Moose River Campground and nearby residences during which residents/owners reported numerous evacuations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A multi-day stretch of hot and humid conditions began on June 18, 2024 and continued through June 20, 2024 across southern Vermont outside of the southern Green Mountains. Heat index (or feels like temperatures) reached between 95 and 104 degrees across the region each day.

A weak mid-level disturbance moved along a stationary boundary along the Canadian-VT border in a very warm and unstable airmass. Scattered thunderstorms started along the international border and then proceeded south and east as the boundary shifted north. Isolated to scattered thunderstorms with wet microburst type wind damage occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and utility lines down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of persistent heavy showers and thunderstorms affected an area from eastern Chittenden, Lamoille, northern Washington and western Caledonia counties during late afternoon on June 23, 2024. Reliable radar estimates and ground truth reports of 2 to 3+ inches of rainfall in a 2-3 hour window, which led to several reports of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding of road shoulders and gravel roads were reported in portions of Washington and county, especially in Worcester, Woodbury and into western Cabot due to 2-3+ inches of rainfall within a 2-3 hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of persistent heavy showers and thunderstorms affected an area from eastern Chittenden, Lamoille, northern Washington and western Caledonia counties during late afternoon on June 23, 2024. Reliable radar estimates and ground truth reports of 2 to 3+ inches of rainfall in a 2-3 hour window, which led to several reports of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A narrow band of 3+ inches of rainfall fell in a 2-3 hour period mainly across the southern portions of Lamoille County, leading to several reports of flash flooding. The most heavily affected area was in the Town of Stowe on Bryan and North Hollow Roads where one bridge washout and notable road damage occurred along the Gold Brook watershed. Report sources included trained spotters, law enforcement and highway departments.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system and potent upper shortwave tracked east from the Great Lakes region, lifting a warm front through southern Vermont on the morning of June 23. With a cold front to the west, southern Vermont fell into the warm sector of the system with warm, moist antecedent conditions contributing to sufficient instability. Higher values of low- and deep-layer shear and steep lapse rates paired with ample forcing from a low level jet gave way to scattered showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon of June 23. Some storms became severe, resulting in a large hail report in Bennington County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of one inch hail 3 miles northeast of Pownal, VT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level ridge began to sink south through southern Vermont on Thursday, June 20, giving way to a surface trough tracking north to south. Several upper-level shortwave disturbances within the flow aloft worked in tandem with this surface feature to contribute forcing for ascent in an environment abundant with instability due to antecedent, higher-than-normal heat and humidity. The result was scattered showers and thunderstorms, some of which became severe in nature in Windham County. ||Severe thunderstorms resulted in several reports of tree and wire damage along several roadways in the town of Vernon. ||Key Impacts: tree damage, wire damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed onto wires near Tyler Hill Road and Hubbard Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major storm across the Great Lakes remained nearly stationary during the afternoon of April 3rd as a coastal low developed off the NJ-DE that eventually moved to near Cape Cod, MA by midday April 4th. In addition, a strong area of high pressure was located in Hudson Bay but was ridging/cresting toward northern New England and NY.||Windy conditions developed across VT during the afternoon hours of April 3rd and continued into the early morning hours of April 4th. The stronger winds were located across southern VT where some wind gusts exceeded 50-60 mph with approximately 13,000+ outages due to wind.||Mixed rain, sleet and snow developed in the afternoon and evening hours of April 3rd and then transitioned to a heavy wet snow overnight through midday on April 4th. Snowfall totals ranged from 2 to 6 inches along the immediate lakeshore with 6 to 10 inches within 3-5 miles in the Champlain valley. Other snowfall totals were 8 to 12 inches within the immediate Connecticut River Valley with 12 to 20+ inches elsewhere across the state.||Impacts were significant across the region with hundreds of motor vehicle accidents, numerous school closing and more than 35.000 power outages at it's peak due to snow weighted trees on powerlines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy, wet snowfall totals of 4 to 18+ inches were common with only a few inches along the immediate lakeshore and 18+ inches in the foothills. Some specific snowfall totals include; 22 inches in Underhill, 19 inches in Westford, 15 inches in Williston, 14 inches in Milton, 10 inches at  the NWS office in South Burlington, 9 inches in Essex and 5 inches in Burlington and Winooski.

The stronger winds were located across southern VT where some wind gusts exceeded 50-60 mph with approximately 13,000+ outages due to wind.||Mixed rain, sleet and snow developed in the afternoon and evening hours of April 3rd and then transitioned to a heavy wet snow overnight through midday on April 4th. Snowfall totals ranged from 2 to 6 inches along the immediate lakeshore with 6 to 10 inches within 3-5 miles in the Champlain valley. Other snowfall totals were 8 to 12 inches within the immediate Connecticut River Valley with 12 to 20+ inches elsewhere across the state.||Impacts were significant across the region with hundreds of motor vehicle accidents, numerous school closing and more than 35.000 power outages at it's peak due to snow weighted trees on powerlines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy, wet snowfall totals of 5 to 8 inches were common with nearly a trace along the immediate lakeshore. Some specific snowfall totals include; 8 inches in Alburgh and 6 inches in North Hero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent Miller Type B storm system (weakening low pressure to the west over the Great Lakes and strengthening low off the east coast) brought locally strong to damaging wind gusts, rain, and heavy, wet snow to southern Vermont over the period of April 3-5, 2024. The greatest snowfall totals accumulated at the higher peaks of the Southern Green Mountains where totals ranged from 7 to 12 inches with locally higher accumulations of 13 to 16 inches at their highest peaks. Lower elevations of Bennington and Windham County generally received about 2 to 6 inches.||This storm also produced strong to locally damaging wind gusts across southern Vermont. Gusts ranging from 40 to 60 mph were common with locally stronger gusts of 72 mph recorded in Bennington County. This, combined with the snow, lead to numerous power outages and downed trees and wires.||Key Impacts: travel delays, power outages, school closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Zone average snowfall of 7.6 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving upper level disturbance and cold front brought scattered showers and thunderstorms to southern Vermont on August 5, 2024. One storm became severe on this day, yielding a report of several downed trees along Vermont State Route 5 just south of the town of Westminster.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over social media, a member of the public reported multiple trees down on Vermont SR 5 South of Westminster.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On August 9, 2024, remnants of Tropical Storm Debby impacted southern Vermont, bringing periods of moderate to rain and gusty winds. Total rainfall accumulations generally ranged from about 1-2 with non-thunderstorm wind gusts maximizing between about 35 to 50 mph. Gusty winds led to a couple of isolated reports of downed trees and wires in the towns of Dorset and East Dorset.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local emergency manager reported a couple of trees down on a roadway and trees down on wires in the tows of Dorset and East Dorset. The roads impacted include Church Street and Overlook Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Hurricane Debby moved across western and northern NY during the afternoon and eventually across northwest VT during the early evening hours of August 9th. The heaviest rainfall associated with this storm was across northern NY with more than 6 inches of rain. In VT, 1 to 3 inches occurred with the higher amounts across northern sections.||The main impacts for VT were largely across the central and northern Champlain Valleys east to the western slopes of the Green Mountains where south-southeast winds gusted to around 60 mph in some locations with full-leaved trees accounting for numerous tree damage that fell onto utility lines and created more than 50,000 outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured wind gust of 62 mph at Burlington Int'l Airport and other observed or estimated wind gusts greater than 50 mph resulted in numerous reports of downed branches and trees on utility lines, structures and property (Vehicles).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated wind gusts and or local mesonet observations in excess of 50-55 mph across fully leaved trees resulted in numerous reports of downed branches and trees on utility lines, structures and property (Vehicles).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abnormally dry conditions across portions of southern Vermont resulted in the development of severe drought conditions (Severe - D2) across portions of Bennington and Windham counties starting on November 12, 2024 and continuing into the middle to end of December.

A low pressure system tracking from the mid-Atlantic States to eastern New England brought a mix of rain and snow to southern Vermont on Thanksgiving Day, November 28, 2024.||Heavy snowfall set up at elevations above 1000 feet with amounts between 7 and 14 inches common. Lower snowfall amounts (1 to 6 inches) occurred in the lower elevations due to some rain mixing in. This snowfall resulted in snow covered and slippery roads impacting Thanksgiving travel.||Key impacts: travel delays.

A low pressure system tracking from the mid-Atlantic States to eastern New England brought a mix of rain and snow to southern Vermont on Thanksgiving Day, November 28, 2024.||Heavy snowfall set up at elevations above 1000 feet with amounts between 7 and 14 inches common. A potent clipper low pressure system impacted southern Vermont late Wednesday, December 4 through Friday December 6, bringing widespread light to moderate and elevation-dependent, heavy snowfall and gusty winds. The heaviest snow fell in the highest elevations of the Southern Greens where upslope enhancement contributed to moderate to heavy accumulations. Peak wind gusts throughout the region generally reached 30 to 40 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Zone average snowfall of 11.1 inches.
